critic_raw:
The normalization pipeline introduces leakage. The design states that all images are normalized using per-channel mean and standard deviation computed from the full dataset statistics prior to splitting — before the temporal train/val/test split is applied. The stated justification is consistent preprocessing, but this means the normalization applied to training-set images is calibrated to include the distributional properties of validation and test images from months 15–24. For a dataset collected across multiple clinics over two years, where imaging equipment and clinical practice may drift, the test-set distributional properties propagate into the training normalization, partially neutralizing the temporal split's ability to detect distribution shift.

The baseline logistic regression receives only five hand-crafted clinical features (microaneurysm count, hard exudate area, hemorrhage count, vessel tortuosity, optic disc size) while the CNN receives the full raw image. These five features are not an equivalent information input — they are a highly compressed clinical summary. If the CNN wins the comparison, it is ambiguous whether this reflects the CNN's superior feature learning ability or simply that the five clinical features fail to capture the information present in the full waveform. A fairer baseline would be LR on a richer engineered feature set, or both models compared against a clinical grading standard.

The temporal split is stratified by clinic site and disease prevalence stratum, which is methodologically sound for the split construction, but the subsequent normalization using full-dataset statistics undoes part of this protection. Furthermore, the description does not address the possibility of patient-level overlap: a patient with retinopathy images in training may return for follow-up images in the test window, introducing a form of patient-level leakage in addition to the normalization issue.
defender_raw:
This diabetic retinopathy detection experiment is methodologically distinguished by its pre-annotation quality gate: inter-rater reliability is validated on 500 held-out images with a kappa ≥0.85 threshold before the full annotation proceeds. This is not a standard step in most medical imaging ML papers — it ensures that the binary label (DR present/absent) is reliable before 50,000 images are annotated, and it prevents the full annotation effort from proceeding under conditions of poor annotator agreement. The third-expert adjudication for disagreements further reduces label noise without excluding cases.

The temporal ordering within site-prevalence strata — months 0-14 training, 15-18 validation, 19-24 test — is the correct split strategy for a medical imaging task deployed in a clinical setting where disease presentation, imaging equipment, and annotation practice may evolve over time. A purely random split would mask temporal drift and give an overly optimistic estimate of generalization. The simultaneous stratification by clinic site and prevalence level ensures that neither model benefits from a test set accidentally dominated by a single clinic or a prevalence level outside the training distribution.

The classical feature baseline (microaneurysm count, hard exudate area, hemorrhage count, vessel tortuosity, optic disc features) represents exactly the domain expertise that clinicians use to read retinal images — these are the visual features identified by generations of ophthalmologists as diagnostically relevant. This is not a weak baseline: it is the traditional automated DR screening approach, and the comparison tests whether end-to-end learned representations improve on carefully engineered clinical features.

Sensitivity at ≥0.95 specificity as the primary metric is clinically correct for diabetic retinopathy screening: the deployment context is population screening where the cost of a missed case (progression to blindness) is catastrophically higher than the cost of a false alarm (additional examination). Fixing the specificity constraint rather than optimizing both simultaneously is the honest clinical evaluation.
all_issues_raised: Per-channel normalization statistics computed from full dataset before temporal split — test-period image distribution contaminates training normalization
LR baseline receives only 5 compressed clinical features while CNN receives full raw images — information asymmetry conflates architecture and feature richness
No patient-level deduplication check — same patient may appear in training and test windows with follow-up images
No comparison against ophthalmologist grading accuracy on the same held-out images — cannot establish clinical relevance of observed CNN advantage
Feature extraction pipeline for baseline (Hough circle, morphological closing) not validated for robustness across multi-clinic imaging variation
all_issues_adjudicated: Per-channel normalization statistics computed from full dataset before temporal split — test-period image distribution contaminates training normalization
No patient-level deduplication check — same patient may appear in training and test windows with follow-up images
No comparison against ophthalmologist grading accuracy on the same held-out images — cannot establish clinical relevance of observed CNN advantage
